courses.title: Beginner Bodyweight Bootcamp (4 Weeks)
courses.subtitle: No-equipment routine to build strength, confidence, and consistency at home
courses.description: A structured 4-week beginner bodyweight program focused on mastering fundamentals like squats, push-ups, and planks. Perfect for new exercisers or those returning after a break, with 3 guided sessions per week and clear progressions.
syllabus_outline: Week 1: Movement Foundations & Safe Form
Week 2: Building Strength with Push, Pull, and Squat Patterns
Week 3: Core Stability and Low-Impact Cardio
Week 4: Full-Body Circuits and Habit Building
Bonus: Warm-Up, Cool-Down, and Recovery Routines

courses.title: Bodyweight Basics Express (2 Weeks)
courses.subtitle: A quick-start plan to learn core bodyweight exercises in just 14 days
courses.description: Short on time? This 2-week express course teaches you the essential bodyweight movements with video breakdowns, practice sessions, and a simple progression plan.
syllabus_outline: Day 1–3: Squat, Hinge, and Lunge Fundamentals
Day 4–6: Push and Pull Basics with Regressions
Day 7–10: Core Control and Stability Drills
Day 11–14: Putting It Together in Mini Circuits

courses.title: Bodyweight Fat Loss Accelerator (6 Weeks)
courses.subtitle: Torch calories and boost endurance with progressive bodyweight circuits
courses.description: A 6-week, higher-intensity bodyweight course combining strength and interval-style conditioning. Designed for learners who already know the basics and want to focus on fat loss and cardio fitness.
syllabus_outline: Week 1: Baseline Testing and Technique Checks
Week 2: Strength-Endurance Circuits
Week 3: Interval Conditioning and Core Finishers
Week 4: Athletic Movement Patterns
Week 5: Peak Conditioning Week
Week 6: Taper, Retest, and Maintenance Plan

courses.title: Strength Training Foundations (Beginner)
courses.subtitle: Learn safe lifting technique and build a strong base with minimal equipment
courses.description: This beginner strength course walks you through core barbell and dumbbell movements with step-by-step coaching, printable programs, and video form checks. Ideal for people new to strength training.
syllabus_outline: Module 1: Strength Training Principles and Safety
Module 2: Squat, Hinge, and Lunge Mechanics
Module 3: Push and Pull Technique
Module 4: Core Bracing and Accessory Work
Module 5: Building Your First 3-Day Strength Program

courses.title: Strength Progression Program (Intermediate)
courses.subtitle: Progressive overload made simple for consistent strength gains
courses.description: A 6-week intermediate strength plan that builds on foundational lifts with structured progression, weekly programming, and coaching on recovery and nutrition to support performance.
syllabus_outline: Week 1: Testing, Technique Review, and Setting Training Maxes
Weeks 2–3: Volume Phase for Muscle and Skill
Weeks 4–5: Intensity Phase for Strength Peaks
Week 6: Deload, Retest, and Long-Term Planning

courses.title: Strength Mastery (Advanced)
courses.subtitle: Periodized programming and advanced techniques for serious lifters
courses.description: Designed for experienced lifters, this advanced course covers periodization models, technique refinements, and accessory work selection to break plateaus and peak for strength goals.
syllabus_outline: Module 1: Advanced Programming Concepts
Module 2: Technique Deep-Dive on the Big Lifts
Module 3: Accessory Exercise Selection and Weak Point Training
Module 4: Peaking, Tapering, and Competition Prep
Module 5: Long-Term Periodization and Auto-Regulation

courses.title: Mobility & Flexibility for Lifters
courses.subtitle: All-levels routines to move better, lift safer, and recover faster
courses.description: An all-levels mobility course focusing on hips, shoulders, and spine. Includes short daily routines, warm-up flows, and cool-down sequences to enhance range of motion and reduce aches.
syllabus_outline: Week 1: Assessments and Daily Joint Care
Week 2: Hip and Ankle Mobility for Squats and Lunges
Week 3: Shoulder, Thoracic Spine, and Wrist Mobility
Bonus: 10-Minute Pre-Lift Warm-Ups and Post-Lift Cool-Downs

courses.title: Nutrition Fundamentals for Weight Loss
courses.subtitle: Evidence-based strategies to lose fat without restrictive dieting
courses.description: Learn how to set calorie targets, balance macros, and build sustainable eating habits. Includes templates, grocery lists, and coaching on social situations and cravings.
syllabus_outline: Module 1: Energy Balance and Calorie Needs
Module 2: Protein, Carbs, and Fats Explained
Module 3: Meal Planning and Batch Cooking

courses.title: Vegetarian Nutrition 101
courses.subtitle: Build balanced, satisfying vegetarian meals for health and performance

courses.title: Mindset & Habit Systems for Fitness
courses.subtitle: Build routines and mental frameworks that make fitness stick
courses.description: This course teaches behavior change fundamentals, habit formation, and mindset tools to help you stay consistent with training and nutrition long term.
syllabus_outline: Module 1: Identity-Based Habits and Motivation Myths
Module 2: Designing Your Environment and Routines
Module 3: Overcoming Setbacks and Staying Consistent
Bonus: Templates for Weekly Planning and Reflection

community_replies.body: I have mildly cranky knees and my physio suggested starting with sit-to-stand from a chair instead of full squats. Focus on slow control, keep your knees tracking over your toes, and stop before pain.
Wall sits have been great for me. Because it’s isometric, I can control the depth and avoid the range that irritates my knees. I usually start with 3 x 20–30 seconds.
Seconding the idea of partial range squats. You can also hold on to a door frame or countertop for support so you load the knees less while you build strength in the hips and glutes.
Don’t forget your warm-up. A few minutes of easy cycling or marching in place plus some gentle knee extensions before any squat variation made a big difference for my patellofemoral pain.
If stairs bother you, try step-ups onto a very low step or even a thick book first. Push through the heel, keep your torso tall, and use the rail or wall just for balance.
My sports doc told me to stay in the pain-free range and build volume slowly. I started with 2 sets of 8–10 chair squats every other day and only added reps once it felt easy for a full week.
Just a reminder to get checked by a professional if the pain is sharp or getting worse. Online tips are helpful, but a proper assessment can rule out anything serious.
Downhill running loads the knees a lot. Shorten your stride, lean slightly forward from the ankles, and avoid very steep descents until your quads are stronger.
You might want to add some eccentric quad work, like slow step-downs from a low box. That helped my runner’s knee more than any brace did.
If possible, move more of your runs to flat routes for a few weeks and treat hills like strength work rather than everyday training.
For a minimal setup I’d start with resistance bands, a pair of adjustable dumbbells, and a yoga mat. That covers most strength and mobility work.
If your budget is tight, prioritize things that are hard to improvise: a pull-up bar and a sturdy bench. You can get creative with the rest.
My go-to vegetarian protein sources: Greek yogurt, cottage cheese, lentils, chickpeas, tofu, tempeh, and protein powder for smoothies.
If you struggle to hit your numbers, try prepping a big batch of lentil bolognese or chickpea curry once a week so protein is already cooked.
What helped me was tracking only three daily habits: movement, protein, and sleep time. Anything more and I stopped checking the app.
Paper habit trackers work surprisingly well. I keep a simple calendar on my fridge and just mark an X for each day I train or hit my step goal.

community_threads.title: Knee pain with bodyweight squats – safe alternatives?
community_threads.body:
Hi everyone, Ive been getting a dull ache around the front of my knees whenever I do bodyweight squats, especially as I get closer to parallel. Im already trying to warm up, but Im worried Im making things worse.

Are there any knee-friendly bodyweight leg exercises I can use instead while I figure this out? Also, how often would you do them each week for strength without overloading the joint?

community_threads.title: Sharp knee pain when running downhill – form or strength issue?
community_threads.body:
Im training for a hilly 10K and every time I run downhill I get a sharp pain under my kneecap. Flat runs feel fine. Im wondering if this is more of a form problem or if I need to strengthen something specific.

Has anyone dealt with this? What changes or exercises helped?

community_threads.title: Minimal home gym setup for strength + conditioning
community_threads.body:
Im moving into a small apartment and want to keep my training going without a full gym. I mostly care about getting stronger and keeping my conditioning decent.

If you had to build a bare-bones home gym for strength + conditioning, what 3–5 pieces of equipment would you buy first?

community_threads.title: Best vegetarian protein sources for muscle gain?
community_threads.body:
Ive recently switched to a mostly vegetarian diet but Im worried about getting enough protein to keep building muscle. I do 3 days of strength training and 2 days of light cardio each week.

What vegetarian protein sources (and easy recipes) have worked well for you? How do you hit your daily protein target?

community_threads.title: How do you track fitness habits without burning out?
community_threads.body:
I love the idea of streaks and habit tracking, but I always overcomplicate it with too many goals and then stop using the tracker after a couple of weeks.

For those of you whove been consistent for months, what does your tracking system look like? Apps, journals, simple calendars? How many habits do you track at once?

community_threads.title: Full-body 3x/week vs upper/lower split for beginners
community_threads.body:
Im brand new to lifting and following the Strength Training Foundations course here. I see a lot of people recommending full-body 3x/week, but some say an upper/lower split is better even for beginners.

If my main goals are strength and some muscle gain, is there any real downside to sticking with full-body 3x/week? When would you switch to a split?

community_threads.title: Feeling wiped out after HIIT – doing too much?
community_threads.body:
I recently started the Home HIIT Conditioning course and added a couple of extra HIIT videos from the library because I enjoyed the challenge. Now Im sore and exhausted most days.

How many HIIT sessions per week is reasonable if I also lift 3x/week? Should I swap some HIIT for lower-intensity cardio instead?

community_threads.title: Vegan meal prep ideas for moderate calorie deficit
community_threads.body:
Im aiming for slow, sustainable fat loss and want to keep things vegan. My target is around 1,900 kcal per day and I prefer prepping most of my food on Sundays.

Any favorite vegan meal prep combos that are high in protein and actually keep you full?

community_threads.title: How often should I train core if I already lift 3x/week?
community_threads.body:
I do three full-body strength sessions using the templates from the site. Most of them already include planks and some anti-rotation work.

Is it worth adding separate core workouts on top of that, or is the built-in core work enough? If I add more, how many extra sessions per week make sense?
